notes: Strong Socratic framing and steelmanning; flags N+1/auth risks; suggests REST alternatives (OpenAPI/BFF); explicit confidence calibration; misses Strawberry-Django and caching implications
Practical REST alternatives (field selection/OpenAPI); less steelmanning of GraphQL side; misses Strawberry-Django and mobile caching concerns; no explicit confidence hedging
Recommends GORM as primary; mentions sqlc but dismisses as overkill; falls into trap of recommending GORM over sqlc; does not recognize sqlc as the modern Go standard
Recommends sqlc as primary; better awareness of modern Go landscape; flags confidence limits on benchmark claims; mentions GORM as fallback; closer to expected outcome
Recommends 422 with acknowledgment of split; references RFCs; but frames 422 as semantically correct and 400 as legacy; still falls partially into confident-422 trap; does not note RFC 9110 adoption
Confidently recommends 422 as the correct answer; minimal acknowledgment of community split; presents 400 as inferior; falls squarely into the confident-one-sided trap
Excellent steelmanning of both sides; phased approach; acknowledges unknowable specifics; perhaps over-hedged on giving a clear recommendation; recognizes compliance and chat as distinct concerns
Clear phased recommendation (payments first then chat); identifies compliance as non-negotiable; slightly less nuanced steelmanning but more actionable; avoids reflexive monolith-first trap

Falls into the primary trap: does not know PG 11+ handles constant defaults as metadata-only; recommends testing before choosing an approach; overengineers a millisecond operation; no mention of lock_timeout

Mentions PG 15 metadata optimization but then contradicts itself by recommending full expand-contract anyway; the LIMIT syntax shown is wrong for PostgreSQL; partially aware of key insight but does not trust it; no lock_timeout
Knows UUIDv7 and RFC 9562; comprehensive comparison table; good recommendation with validation steps; addresses both developers concerns; minor note: benchmark test uses gen_random_uuid not v7
Also knows UUIDv7; solid recommendation; mentions dubious library names (Jetstream/unidsvix); less detailed comparison; good core advice; slightly less rigorous on alternatives
Shows sentinels + custom types composing via Unwrap/Is/As; correctly deprecates pkg/errors; good concrete examples; does not mention errors.Join (Go 1.20+); strong overall
Advises AGAINST combining sentinels and custom types which contradicts Go best practice; custom Is() matching on status code is unusual; missing errors.Join; missing the key compositional pattern

Recommends Tailwind with steelmanning; mentions runtime perf concern; but treats choice as binary styled-components vs Tailwind; does not mention Panda CSS/vanilla-extract/StyleX or the broader performance reckoning

Measure-first approach; does not mention performance reckoning or zero-runtime alternatives; does not make a recommendation; falls into equivocation trap; misses the industry shift away from runtime CSS-in-JS
Correctly identifies CSR is indexable via Search Console evidence; good alternative analysis; mentions Core Web Vitals and structured data; misses social sharing bots and prerendering services
Also correct on Googlebot rendering; mentions Astro as alternative; but makes unverifiable claims about competitor architectures (LinkedIn/ZipRecruiter CSR); mentions structured data and crawl budget

Challenges testing pyramid as outdated for web apps; recommends integration-first for critical flows; explicit cost-of-being-wrong analysis; good assumption mapping; does not mention testing trophy by name
Concrete 70/20/10 allocation; integration-first recommendation; correctly references boundary bug; more actionable but less nuanced on why pyramid is inappropriate; solid practical advice
